notes.text: The H1 title does not contain any of this week's primary SEO keywords (e.g. 'fintech AI', 'AI risk management finance', 'AI trading algorithms'). Retitle to something like 'Fintech AI Regulatory Gap: How Agentic Systems Are Outpacing Compliance Frameworks in 2026' to capture target keyword traffic without sacrificing editorial voice.

The Sources section lists only two URLs (Taylor Wessing and FinTech Futures), but the article body cites at least five named sources — FinRegLab, Debevoise & Plimpton, Hogan Lovells, Braithwaite, and Klarna company filings. Every inline citation must have a corresponding URL in the Sources section; missing links undermine credibility and prevent editors from verifying claims.

The stat-box citing Forbes contributor Zenon Kapron ('5% of intraday liquidity buffers... by year-end 2025') is a single-contributor Forbes piece, not a primary research source. For a statistic this specific and consequential, cite the underlying research report directly, or add a qualifying phrase such as 'per Forbes contributor Zenon Kapron, citing [underlying source]' to flag its secondary-source nature to readers.

This article covers AI Investment Strategy / Capital Markets, which the Marketing Director has explicitly flagged as off-limits this week ('we do not yet have enough domain authority to compete in those SERPs — focus depth over breadth'). Before writing, always check the 'Content Pillar Focus This Week' section of the SEO guidelines. This week's assignment is AI in Finance Operations: fraud detection, credit scoring, and compliance automation. An otherwise well-written article was rejected solely because it targeted the wrong pillar at the wrong time.

The H1 ('Morgan Stanley's 2026 AI Breakthrough: What It Means for Tech Valuations') contains neither the required primary keyword nor follows the SEO H1 formula ([Primary Keyword]: [Named Entity] + [Specific Context]). The closest applicable primary keyword would be 'AI investment strategy' — the H1 should read something like 'AI Investment Strategy 2026: Morgan Stanley's Compute-vs-Software Forecast.' Always open the SEO guidelines and match the H1 to a keyword from Section 2 before finalizing the headline.

The Sources section lists five entries without URLs (Yahoo Finance, RBC Analysis, TradingView, Capital Group, Software Equity Group). Editorial standards require all URLs used in research to be listed at the bottom. Partial citations erode reader trust and make fact-checking impossible. Either find and include the direct URL, or restructure the inline citation to name the publication and report title precisely enough that a reader could locate it independently — e.g., 'Software Equity Group 2026 Annual SaaS Report, published March 2026.'

The H1 ('Big Tech's $700B AI Bet Strains Power Grids and Carbon Markets') is strong editorially but does not contain the primary SEO keyword 'AI data center energy costs' as required by the Marketing Director's H1 formula. On your next article, fit the target keyword into the H1 naturally — for example: 'AI Data Center Energy Costs: How Big Tech's $690B Bet Is Straining Power Grids and Carbon Markets.' You lose nothing in clarity and gain the SEO signal.

The internal link to 'fintech AI infrastructure investment' uses a placeholder URL (https://example.com/fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment) instead of the real published slug. Before submission, always replace placeholder hrefs with the actual relative slug paths defined in the SEO guidelines — in this case, /fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment. A broken internal link erodes both SEO equity and reader trust.

The article covers three distinct financial audiences — CFOs managing ESG-linked debt, utility sector investors, and AI infrastructure planners — but the conclusion addresses all three at once without differentiation. Consider flagging audience-specific takeaways with a brief label (e.g., 'For CFOs:' / 'For utility investors:') to make the piece more forwardable to a specific colleague, which is a key behavior for our C-suite readership.
